content.text: What is AutoGen Studio?

AutoGen Studio is a no-code / low-code web interface for building, testing, and iterating on AutoGen agent teams. It runs as a local server and exposes a browser UI where you can define agents, assemble them into teams, set model configurations and tools, and chat with the team interactively — all without writing any Python. Under the hood it uses the same
autogen-agentchat
runtime described in the rest of this module.

attrs.alt: AutoGen Studio: Browser UI talks to a FastAPI+WebSocket Studio Server, which persists teams and sessions to SQLite and spawns an autogen-agentchat runtime to execute them

content.text: Installing and Starting AutoGen Studio
AutoGen Studio is a separate package. Install it alongside
autogen-agentchat
and start the server with a single command.

pip install autogenstudio autogen-agentchat autogen-ext[openai]

# Start the server (default port 8080)
autogenstudio ui --port 8080

# Open http://localhost:8080 in your browser
Python requirement:
AutoGen Studio requires Python 3.10+. It stores teams, sessions, and messages in a local SQLite database at ~/.autogenstudio/database.sqlite by default. Pass
--appdir
to use a different directory.
Key Concepts in the Studio UI
UI section
Purpose
Models
Define LLM model configurations (provider, model name, API key, base URL)
Tools
Register Python tools by pasting or linking code — the studio wraps them as callable tools
Agents
Create agent configurations: type, model, system message, tools, and description
Teams
Assemble agents into a team: choose team type (RoundRobin, Selector, Swarm), set termination conditions
Playground
Chat with a selected team interactively, inspect message traces, and download sessions
Defining a Team in the Studio

The Studio uses a declarative JSON schema to represent teams. You can edit teams in the UI or export and import them as JSON files to version control your team configurations. A team JSON includes agent definitions, model configs, and termination conditions all in one file.
Loading a Studio Team in Python
Teams defined in AutoGen Studio can be loaded directly in Python using
TeamManager
or the
autogenstudio
Python API. This lets you prototype in the UI and then execute the same team configuration in a script or production service.

import asyncio
from autogenstudio.teammanager import TeamManager

async def main():
    manager = TeamManager()

    # Run the team by ID (shown in the Studio UI)
    result = await manager.run(
        task="Research and implement merge sort in Python.",
        team_id="<your-team-id-from-studio>",
    )

    for msg in result.messages:
        print(f"[{msg.source}] {str(msg.content)[:100]}")

asyncio.run(main())
Exporting and Importing Team Configs
Export a team from the Studio UI as a JSON file and load it programmatically with
ComponentLoader
. This is useful for deploying team configurations to production without copying agent code.

import asyncio
import json
from autogen_agentchat.base import TaskRunner
from autogenstudio.utils.component_config_utils import ComponentLoader

async def main():
    # Load team from an exported JSON file
    with open("my_team.json") as f:
        team_config = json.load(f)

    team = ComponentLoader.load_component(team_config)
    result = await team.run(task="Write a binary search function in Python.")

    for msg in result.messages:
        print(f"[{msg.source}] {str(msg.content)[:100]}")

asyncio.run(main())
Studio vs Code: When to Use Each
Task
Studio
Python code
Rapid prototyping / experimenting
Yes — visual, no code required
Slower — write and iterate code
Sharing agent configs with non-developers
Yes — export JSON or share UI link
Requires Python setup
Production automation pipelines
Limited — no programmatic control flow
Yes — full control with asyncio, APIs
Custom agent logic (subclassing)
No
Yes — BaseAgent or AssistantAgent subclass
Version control of team configs
Yes — export JSON to git
Yes — Python files in git
Debugging message traces
Yes — built-in trace viewer
Yes — inspect result.messages
Recommended workflow:
use AutoGen Studio to prototype and validate your team design quickly, then export the JSON config and load it in production Python code via
ComponentLoader
. This gives you the speed of the visual UI without sacrificing production control.
AutoGen Studio CLI Reference

# Start on a custom port
autogenstudio ui --port 8090

# Use a specific app data directory
autogenstudio ui --appdir /path/to/my-studio-data

# Serve without opening the browser
autogenstudio ui --no-open

# Check the installed version
autogenstudio version
Registering Custom Tools in Studio

In the Studio UI, navigate to Tools and create a new tool by pasting a Python function. The function must have type-annotated parameters and a docstring — these become the tool's JSON schema that agents use. Studio stores the function source and executes it in a sandboxed subprocess when an agent calls it during a session.
Session History and Replay

Every conversation in the Studio Playground is saved as a session. Click any past session in the left sidebar to replay the full message trace, including tool calls and code execution outputs. Sessions are stored in the local SQLite database and can be exported as JSON for offline review or sharing.

# Sessions can also be accessed programmatically via the Studio database:
import sqlite3
import json

db_path = "~/.autogenstudio/database.sqlite"
import os
db_path = os.path.expanduser(db_path)

if os.path.exists(db_path):
    conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    # List recent sessions
    cursor = conn.execute(
        "SELECT id, name, created_at FROM session ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5"
    )
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        print(f"Session {row[0]}: {row[1]} ({row[2]})")
    conn.close()
else:
    print("Studio database not found — start AutoGen Studio first.")
Environment Variables for Model Keys
Never paste raw API keys into the Studio UI or the team JSON config. Instead, use environment variable references. AutoGen Studio resolves values prefixed with
as environment variable lookups at runtime.

# Set the key in your shell before starting Studio
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

# In the Studio Model config, set api_key to:
#   $OPENAI_API_KEY
# Studio substitutes the value at runtime.

autogenstudio ui --port 8080
Security:
even with env var substitution, the Studio database stores model configurations. Keep the database file out of version control and restrict its permissions (
chmod 600 ~/.autogenstudio/database.sqlite
) on shared machines.
Running Studio in a Docker Container
For team environments or reproducible deployments, run AutoGen Studio in Docker. Mount a host directory for persistence so sessions and team configs survive container restarts.

# Build a simple image
docker run -it --rm \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -v $(pwd)/studio-data:/root/.autogenstudio \
  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=$OPENAI_API_KEY \
  python:3.11-slim \
  bash -c "pip install autogenstudio autogen-agentchat autogen-ext[openai] && autogenstudio ui --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080"

# Open http://localhost:8080
Debugging Teams in the Playground

The Studio Playground shows a real-time message trace alongside the chat. Each message card shows the sender, content, message type (text, tool call, tool result), and timestamp. Use this view to diagnose:
Wrong agent selection: check which agent the team picked and compare with the agent descriptions.
Tool call failures: the tool result message shows the full error if the tool raised an exception.
Premature termination: if the team stopped before the task was done, inspect which message triggered the termination condition.
Loops: if the same agent responds repeatedly, check the team type (RoundRobin may loop by design) and the max_round or MaxMessageTermination setting.
Automating Studio via the REST API

AutoGen Studio exposes a REST API that the browser UI uses internally. You can call it directly to create teams, start sessions, and retrieve messages programmatically — useful for CI pipelines or automated evaluation.

BASE=http://localhost:8080/api

# List all teams
curl -s $BASE/teams | python3 -m json.tool | head -40

# Run a task on a team (replace TEAM_ID with the real id)
curl -s -X POST $BASE/sessions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"team_id": "TEAM_ID", "name": "test-session"}' | python3 -m json.tool

# Stream a task result (WebSocket endpoint)
# ws://localhost:8080/api/ws/sessions/SESSION_ID/run
Limitations of AutoGen Studio
No programmatic control flow: Studio runs one task at a time interactively; it cannot loop over a dataset or branch based on tool results.
Single-machine only: Studio uses a local SQLite database and runs agents in a single process. It is not designed for distributed or multi-tenant deployments.
Custom agent subclasses: agents that extend BaseAgent or override handle_message() cannot be defined in the Studio UI.
No streaming to external systems: conversation output streams to the browser but cannot be wired to external sinks (Kafka, webhooks) without custom code.
Versioning Team Configs with Git

Because Studio exports team configs as plain JSON, you can track them in git like any other configuration file. A typical workflow keeps the Studio database for local iteration but commits only the exported JSON configs to the repository — other team members import them into their local Studio instance.

# 1. In Studio UI: Export team -> save to teams/research-team.json
# 2. Commit the config
git add teams/research-team.json
git commit -m "chore: update research team agent descriptions"

# 3. On another machine: import via Studio UI (Settings -> Import Team)
#    OR load programmatically:
python3 - <<'PY'
import asyncio, json
from autogenstudio.utils.component_config_utils import ComponentLoader

async def main():
    with open("teams/research-team.json") as f:
        config = json.load(f)
    team = ComponentLoader.load_component(config)
    result = await team.run(task="Summarise the AutoGen 0.4 release notes.")
    print(result.stop_reason)

asyncio.run(main())
PY
Studio Workflow: End-to-End Example
A typical Studio session for building a research-and-code team:
Models: add an OpenAI model config with your API key stored in $OPENAI_API_KEY.
Tools: paste a web_search function that calls a search API and returns JSON results.
Agents: create 'researcher' (AssistantAgent, web_search tool) and 'coder' (AssistantAgent, no tools).
Teams: create a SelectorGroupChat with researcher and coder, TextMentionTermination('TERMINATE'), max 15 messages.
Playground: enter the task 'Research merge sort and implement it in Python. End with TERMINATE.' and observe the trace.
Export: download the team JSON and commit it to git for reproducibility.
Summary
AutoGen Studio is a local web UI for building and testing AutoGen teams without writing Python.
Install with pip install autogenstudio and start with autogenstudio ui.
The UI covers Models, Tools, Agents, Teams, and a Playground for interactive sessions.
Teams are stored as declarative JSON configs that can be exported, version-controlled, and loaded in Python.
Load Studio team configs in code via TeamManager.run() or ComponentLoader.load_component() for production use.
Use Studio for prototyping and visual inspection; use Python code for production pipelines requiring custom logic.
